data.title: Favourable Signals For Invert Graphite: Numerous Insiders Acquired Stock
data.description:
It is usually uneventful when a single insider buys stock. However, When quite a few insiders buy shares, as it happened in Invert Graphite Limited's (ASX:IVG) case, it's fantastic news for shareholders.

While insider transactions are not the most important thing when it comes to long-term investing, we would consider it foolish to ignore insider transactions altogether.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Invert Graphite Insider Transactions Over The Last Year

In the last twelve months, the biggest single purchase by an insider was when insider David Brookes bought AU$75k worth of shares at a price of AU$0.03 per share. That means that an insider was happy to buy shares at above the current price of AU$0.03. While their view may have changed since the purchase was made, this does at least suggest they have had confidence in the company's future. In our view, the price an insider pays for shares is very important. As a general rule, we feel more positive about a stock if insiders have bought shares at above current prices, because that suggests they viewed the stock as good value, even at a higher price.

While Invert Graphite insiders bought shares during the last year, they didn't sell. You can see a visual depiction of insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last 12 months, below. If you click on the chart, you can see all the individual transactions, including the share price, individual, and the date!

See our latest analysis for Invert Graphite

There are plenty of other companies that have insiders buying up shares. You probably do not want to miss this free list of undervalued small cap companies that insiders are buying.

Insider Ownership Of Invert Graphite

Another way to test the alignment between the leaders of a company and other shareholders is to look at how many shares they own. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. It appears that Invert Graphite insiders own 28% of the company, worth about AU$3.5m. While this is a strong but not outstanding level of insider ownership, it's enough to indicate some alignment between management and smaller shareholders.

So What Do The Invert Graphite Insider Transactions Indicate?

The fact that there have been no Invert Graphite insider transactions recently certainly doesn't bother us. However, our analysis of transactions over the last year is heartening. Overall we don't see anything to make us think Invert Graphite insiders are doubting the company, and they do own shares. So while it's helpful to know what insiders are doing in terms of buying or selling, it's also helpful to know the risks that a particular company is facing. Our analysis shows 6 warning signs for Invert Graphite (5 are a bit unpleasant!) and we strongly recommend you look at these before investing.

But note: Invert Graphite may not be the best stock to buy. So take a peek at this free list of interesting companies with high ROE and low debt.

For the purposes of this article, insiders are those individuals who report their transactions to the relevant regulatory body. We currently account for open market transactions and private dispositions of direct interests only, but not derivative transactions or indirect interests.

data.title: Ceasefire Eases Fuel Costs And Reshapes The UPS Profit Story
data.description:
• A ceasefire in the Strait of Hormuz is allowing vessels to pass more freely, easing recent disruptions in oil flows.
  • Crude oil prices have pulled back, reducing fuel costs for global shippers including United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS).
  • Lower fuel expenses improve UPS's operating environment and may support stronger profit margins versus the recent conflict period.

For United Parcel Service, the shift in fuel costs comes at a time when the stock has shown mixed performance, with a 3.6% gain over the past week and an 11.9% return over the past year, but weaker results over the past 3 and 5 years. With shares at $101.7, this external relief on fuel gives investors a fresh angle to consider alongside the company’s longer term return profile.

Lower crude prices feed directly into UPS's cost structure through reduced fuel and potentially more stable surcharges, which can support profit margins if other costs stay contained. For you as an investor, the key question is how lasting this ceasefire and cost relief proves to be and how effectively UPS converts a friendlier fuel backdrop into consistent earnings quality.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for United Parcel Service by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on United Parcel Service.

3 things going right for United Parcel Service that this headline doesn't cover.

The ceasefire in the Strait of Hormuz and the resulting pullback in crude prices give UPS some breathing room just as it is working through higher labor and restructuring costs. Lower fuel outlays support parcel and freight margins, especially where contracts include fuel surcharges that tend to lag spot price moves. That matters for UPS because recent commentary around earnings points to pressure on profitability and cautious analyst expectations, even as the company progresses with its network overhaul and job reduction plans. The relief on fuel can help offset less flexible labor costs following the Teamsters settlement, where UPS agreed to cap early retirement buyouts and restrict additional severance tools until 2028. For you, the key question is whether this easing in fuel costs proves long enough for UPS to bed down its network changes without needing further pricing actions that could test customers, particularly against competitors such as FedEx and Deutsche Post DHL that are also benefiting from cheaper fuel.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The United Parcel Service Narrative

  • Cheaper fuel supports the narrative’s focus on cost savings and margin improvement, giving UPS more room to benefit from its network reconfiguration and automation efforts while it shifts away from lower margin Amazon volumes.
  • If fuel costs stay softer while parcel pricing and surcharges reset, the expected benefit from cost cuts in the narrative could be harder to separate from temporary external tailwinds, which may complicate how you judge execution quality.
  • The narrative highlights labor, network and volume risks in detail, but the specific scenario of conflict driven fuel spikes giving way to a ceasefire and rapid cost relief is not fully captured and could change the timing of margin outcomes.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for United Parcel Service to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Fuel costs are tied to geopolitical events, so the benefit from the ceasefire could reverse quickly if tensions flare again in key shipping routes.
  • ⚠️ Lower fuel expenses may ease near term pressure, but they do not remove earnings risks already highlighted by analysts, including expectations for weaker upcoming results and labor constraints from the Teamsters agreement.
  • 🎁 A lower fuel bill can support UPS’s effort to stabilize margins while it implements building closures, job reductions and mix changes toward higher margin freight and healthcare logistics.
  • 🎁 If UPS captures the fuel savings more effectively than peers such as FedEx and Deutsche Post DHL, it could improve its relative competitiveness on pricing and service quality.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how UPS talks about fuel in its next few earnings updates, especially the balance between lower fuel expense and adjustments to fuel surcharges. Pay attention to whether management attributes any margin changes to operational progress versus external cost relief, and how that lines up with analyst expectations for earnings declines and later recovery. It is also worth tracking how customers respond if UPS adjusts pricing or surcharges more slowly than fuel moves, as competitors may use that gap to compete for volume.

Approach 1: Rhythm Pharmaceuticals Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts those back to today’s dollars to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For Rhythm Pharmaceuticals, the latest twelve month free cash flow (FCF) is a loss of $157.40 million. Analyst estimates and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to FCF of $725.33 million in 2030, with interim projections moving from a loss in 2026 and 2027 to positive FCF from 2028 onward. These figures are all in US$ and use a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, where the first years follow more detailed forecasts and later years are extrapolated.

When all projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $426.55 per share. Against the current share price of US$86.13, this implies the stock is 79.8% undervalued based on these inputs and assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

For companies where profits are still limited or losses are present, the P/S ratio is often more useful than P/E. It compares the market value directly with current revenue rather than earnings that may be negative or volatile.

In general, higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/S ratio, while slower growth or greater uncertainty usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple.
